topic: Async - JoinSet for Dynamic Task Sets

turns.content: I'm looking at the new codebase and I'm confused about why we're using tokio::task::JoinSet instead of just spawning tasks individually. It feels like it should be simpler to just use a vector of handles, right? Why does this dynamic approach even make sense when my current job uses standard futures?

Great question. The core difference is that JoinSet acts like a single synchronization point for an entire collection of tasks, whereas a vector of handles gives you backends with no built-in coordination mechanism. Think of it like this: if you have a fleet of drones flying in formation, using JoinSet is like having one ground control tower that can instantly tell all the drones to stop and report their status together without needing to send individual radio messages to each drone. With a vector of handles, you'd need to manage every single connection manually, which gets messy quickly when tasks are added or removed dynamically.
Okay so I can add tasks on the fly with this? Like if a task fails, does it automatically remove the rest from the set?

Exactly. That's one of its main strengths - you can abort remaining tasks when an error occurs by calling the interrupt method on the JoinSet itself. It's like pulling a plug on the entire formation; all other drones stop immediately without needing individual commands. This is perfect for scenarios where you need to cancel pending work based on a single failure condition.
Wait, so if I have ten tasks running and one fails, can I just call abort() and they all stop?

Yes, that's exactly how it works. You call the interrupt method on the JoinSet object after detecting an error in one task. It signals cancellation to every pending task in the set simultaneously without needing individual handles or complex state management.
I see, so this is basically replacing my manual wait loops? Does it handle waiting for completions automatically?

Yes, the JoinSet handles both adding tasks and waiting for their completion in one unified interface. You can add new tasks to the set at runtime as needed, then simply wait on the entire collection until all tasks complete or are aborted.

topic: Async - JoinSet for Dynamic Task Sets

turns.content: I'm building a side project where I need to spawn tasks dynamically and wait for the next completion, but sometimes I want to abort all remaining tasks on an error. How do I handle this with JoinSet?

You can use tokio::sync::JoinSet to manage dynamic task sets because it allows you to add new futures at runtime and check if any have completed using the next method. However, simply calling join_next won't abort the remaining tasks; you need to manually iterate through pending futures with remove_all or filter them out before re-adding the cleaned set back into JoinSet.

Wait, that's not how it works. If I call join_next and get an error from one task, the other pending tasks stay in the JoinSet but don't get notified until their own futures complete, right? So to abort them immediately, I have to manually remove all remaining futures using a different approach like cloning the set or using a separate channel to signal cancellation.

That's correct. The error from join_next doesn't automatically cancel other tasks because JoinSet only tracks completion events via the future itself; it doesn't propagate errors across pending items unless you explicitly handle them. To abort remaining tasks, you'd need to manually iterate over the set using a loop that collects futures into a Vec, then call remove_all on those futures before re-adding the cleaned set back into JoinSet.

But wait, I can't just call remove_all because that would block until all futures complete. Is there a way to unblock by adding new tasks while removing old ones? Or do I need to use a different pattern like tokio::spawn with manual cancellation via JoinHandle instead of relying on the JoinSet's internal state management?

You're right that remove_all blocks until completion, so you can't mix removal and addition in one step. Instead, spawn each task as a separate JoinHandle into a Vec, then periodically check those handles with select! or try_join to see if any are ready or errored out. When an error occurs, collect all active JoinHandles, cancel them via the handle's abort method, then clear the set and re-add new tasks.

So using JoinSet directly isn't ideal for this specific use case because it lacks built-in cancellation support? I should probably just track handles in a Vec and manually manage their lifecycle instead of relying on the JoinSet's internal queue mechanism?

Exactly. For your use case where you need dynamic addition and immediate abortion, tracking JoinHandles in a Vec is more flexible than JoinSet because you can inspect each handle independently without waiting for completion. You'd call join_next only when needed to check status, then manually cancel any errored or pending tasks via their respective handles before adding new ones.

Okay, that makes sense. So the core pattern is: spawn tasks as JoinHandles in a Vec, poll them with try_join or select!, and when an error hits, abort all others by iterating through the handles and calling cancel on each one? And then re-add new tasks to the set?

Yes, that's the right approach. You maintain a Vec of JoinHandles, periodically check their status using join_next or try_join, and when an error occurs, you iterate over all active handles, call cancel on each to abort remaining tasks, then clear the list and add new JoinHandles for fresh tasks. This gives you full control over dynamic task lifecycle without relying on JoinSet's limitations.

topic: Async - JoinSet for Dynamic Task Sets

turns.content: I used to use threads in my old project where I'd add tasks to a queue and have them run until done, but now with async Rust it feels like I need a different pattern. Can you explain how I should handle dynamic task additions instead of using thread pools?

Think of tokio's JoinSet like a signal box for trains; instead of having a fixed number of tracks, the JoinSet allows new tasks to enter dynamically while tracking which ones are still running so you can wait for them all or cancel them as needed. For example, if you create a JoinSet with 10 slots and then add more tasks later, those extra tasks will join the set automatically without needing to manage individual handles manually.
Okay, but I thought once a task is added it would just run forever? How do I actually wait for them or stop some of them if one fails?

You're right that they keep running until completion, but the magic happens when you call drain or select on the JoinSet to collect results and remove completed tasks from consideration. If a task panics inside its closure, that failure propagates back through the JoinSet's error channel so you can catch it before draining the rest.

Wait, if I get an error from one task, does that automatically stop all the others? Because my old code had me manually checking each thread and killing them on failure which felt inefficient. Can I just abort everyone else on a single crash?

Not quite; a panic in JoinSet doesn't immediately abort remaining tasks unless you explicitly cancel the whole set using CancelHandle or select with a timeout to stop pending work. However, if you're using tokio's runtime and want to bail out entirely after one failure, you can call the cancellation handle on the entire JoinSet which will interrupt all currently running futures at once.
I'm confused about how exactly I do that cancellation; my brain is foggy from the five years away. Can you walk me through adding a task dynamically and then aborting them?

Imagine your JoinSet as a parking lot where cars represent tasks; when a car arrives (new task), it gets a spot, but if one car crashes (panic), you can either let others continue or signal the whole lot to clear out. To abort all remaining tasks after an error, you'd first capture the JoinSet's CancelHandle via join_set.cancel_handle() and then call cancel on that handle before calling drain to remove completed ones.
I tried adding a task dynamically but my program just hangs forever waiting for something to finish. I think I misunderstood how to actually add tasks to this dynamic structure properly?

You're likely creating the JoinSet without enough slots or not calling drain correctly; instead of manually polling each future, you should create a JoinSet with initial capacity like 10 and then use join_set.add(future) for new tasks. After adding several tasks, call drain() to collect results into a Vec<JoinResult> so your program can proceed without blocking forever.

Wait, I thought the task would be added to the pool once and run until completion automatically? Why do I need to manually add them after creation? Isn't that what JoinSet is for? Am I doing it wrong or is my understanding broken?

That's a common misconception; the JoinSet isn't auto-populating like a thread pool. You must explicitly call join_set.add(future) each time you want to add a new dynamic task, otherwise the set stays empty and drain does nothing. Think of it as filling buckets before pouring them out; without adding futures first, your program waits indefinitely because there's nothing to collect.
I'm getting panics when I call drain on my JoinSet because some tasks are still running? How do I make sure the ones that aren't finished don't cause this crash?

drain() only collects completed futures, so unfinished tasks simply won't appear in the results Vec; they remain in the set until you explicitly cancel them. If you want to avoid panics from incomplete work, check which tasks are still pending before draining or use select with a timeout to interrupt long-running ones instead of forcing an immediate abort.
Can I just keep adding tasks forever without setting a limit on how many? And if one fails, can I gracefully shut down the whole system using JoinSet features?

Yes, you can add unlimited tasks to a JoinSet as long as your runtime handles memory; there's no hard cap built into the type itself. For graceful shutdown after failures, capture the CancelHandle from join_set.cancel_handle() and call cancel on it before draining, which will interrupt all pending futures so your application exits cleanly without waiting for stuck tasks.

One last thing I need clarity on: if I add a task that takes forever, does JoinSet just wait indefinitely until the app crashes? Or is there a way to make it timeout automatically like some other libraries?

JoinSet itself doesn't auto-timeout individual tasks, but you can wrap each future in select with a timeout using tokio::time::timeout so that if any task exceeds its limit, the JoinSet will detect completion early and drain safely. This way you prevent indefinite hanging even if one task runs forever, giving you controlled cancellation behavior instead of letting the system crash.

topic: Async - JoinSet for Dynamic Task Sets

turns.content: I'm stuck on a code review issue where we need to handle dynamic task registration in our tokio runtime. The current approach using simple futures doesn't let us abort remaining tasks when one fails, so I'm considering the JoinSet pattern but I don't understand how it handles dynamic additions without blocking the executor.

Think of the JoinSet like a manager who can fire any employee on the team instantly without waiting for everyone to finish their current job. When you add tasks to the JoinSet, they're queued and processed concurrently by the tokio runtime's threads. If a task fails inside the JoinSet, you call cancel() on that handle immediately, which tells the runtime to stop executing it and cleans up resources efficiently.

That analogy helps with cancellation, but I'm still confused about how we can register tasks dynamically while waiting for completions. Can you show me a concrete example of using the JoinSet to add a task mid-stream without breaking the async flow?

You create a single JoinSet instance at startup and reuse it throughout your application lifecycle. Inside your async function, you call join_set.add() with a closure that captures local state like counters or configuration. This allows you to register new work items on demand. When using the JoinSet, you iterate through pending handles in a loop calling wait_for_completion(), which returns the first task that finishes and lets you process it before adding another one.
I see how we register tasks with closures, but what happens if I add too many concurrent tasks? Does the JoinSet limit the number of active futures based on runtime capacity or thread count?

The JoinSet itself doesn't impose a hard cap like a semaphore; instead, it relies on the underlying tokio runtime's scheduler to manage concurrency. If your system has enough worker threads and I/O capacity, you can add many tasks dynamically. However, if you exceed the runtime's thread budget or create too many blocking operations inside closures, you might hit performance limits, so monitoring actual thread usage is important.

Thanks for clarifying the concurrency model. One last thing though, what happens if I want to abort ALL remaining tasks in the JoinSet at once when a critical error occurs? Is there an API call that does this cleanly?

Yes, you can use join_set.cancel_all() which immediately cancels every pending handle and returns their results. This is the proper way to abort all remaining tasks in a single operation without manually iterating over handles or risking deadlocks.

topic: Async - JoinSet for Dynamic Task Sets

turns.content: I thought if I use a regular channel to send tasks, the async runtime would automatically handle stopping them when an error occurs. Why does the documentation say I need JoinSet specifically? I'm frustrated because my current approach keeps spawning background threads that never die.

The confusion comes from assuming the standard library's channels are sufficient for dynamic task management in Tokio, but they lack the abort capability you need. The real reason is that a static channel cannot track which spawned futures have completed or failed without manual polling; JoinSet was designed to solve this by wrapping multiple futures and waiting for completion while allowing you to cancel all remaining ones with join_abort().

Wait, so I'm manually tracking failures? That sounds inefficient. Can't I just check the error result of each task individually instead of using some special structure like JoinSet? It feels like overengineering.

You are correct that checking individual results works for a fixed set, but it fails when tasks arrive dynamically because you cannot predict how many will exist at runtime. The core problem is that without JoinSet, there is no built-in way to tell the async runtime to stop waiting on all pending futures and cancel them immediately upon an error; manual polling requires extra complexity that JoinSet avoids by design.

Okay, so I should use JoinSet because it handles dynamic addition better than a regular channel. But how do I actually add tasks dynamically without breaking the flow? Is there a specific pattern for this in Tokio?

Yes, you create a single instance of tokio::task::JoinSet and call its insert() method to push new futures into it. The key intuition is that JoinSet acts as a centralized queue where every inserted task shares the same cancellation signal; when you encounter an error in one task and call join_abort(), the runtime stops tracking any remaining uncompleted tasks, preventing orphaned threads from lingering unnecessarily.
